planting.note: Sow seeds in fall to allow natural warm-then-cold stratification over winter; seeds require both phases to break dormancy and will sprout the following spring.

directions: Collect seeds in autumn as soon as the berries ripen and sow immediately in a prepared bed or seed flat at a depth of about 1/4 inch, then allow winter temperatures to provide the necessary stratification sequence. Seeds will germinate in spring once soil warms; transplant seedlings to a permanent site in their second year when they have developed a robust root system. Choose a cool, moist site with well-drained, acidic soil and full sun to partial shade, reflecting this tree's preference for northern forests and high-elevation ridges. Consistent moisture during establishment is critical, as young trees are drought-sensitive; once established, they are exceptionally cold-hardy and low-maintenance.

planting.note: Sow freshly cleaned seeds in fall to undergo natural cold stratification; germination will occur the following spring after the chilling requirement is satisfied.

directions: Sow cleaned seeds directly into a nursery bed in autumn, covering lightly with 1/4 inch of soil; the distinctive white-felted leaves of seedlings make them easy to identify and separate from weeds in spring. Whitebeam is notably tolerant of shallow, alkaline, and chalky soils, making it one of the few ornamental fruiting trees suited to difficult chalk downland conditions. Grow in full sun for the most compact habit and the best berry production; established trees are drought-tolerant and require little supplemental watering except during prolonged dry spells. Allow five or more years from seed for the tree to reach flowering size and begin producing its scarlet autumn berries.

planting.note: Sow seeds immediately after harvest in fall; seeds require extended cold stratification and germination may be uneven, sometimes occurring over two seasons.

directions: Harvest fruits in late autumn and clean the seeds free of pulp before sowing 1/4 inch deep in a nursery bed; expect germination to be slow and potentially staggered over one to two springs, as wild service tree seeds are notoriously variable in their dormancy. Grow in well-drained loamy or clay soil in full sun to light shade, as this species thrives on the woodland edge in its native European habitat. The russet-brown berries are astringent when first ripe but become sweet and date-like after frost or bletting; harvest after the first hard frost for the best flavor. This rare woodland indicator species is long-lived and slow-growing, rewarding patience with outstanding maple-like autumn foliage and excellent value for pollinators and wildlife.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall immediately after cleaning to allow natural warm-then-cold stratification; the two-phase dormancy requires both a warm period and a cold period before germination will occur.

directions: Clean seeds from ripe black fruits in late summer and sow immediately to take advantage of the warm-soil period before winter cold sets in; this two-phase stratification requirement is essential and skipping either phase will delay germination by a full year. Plant in moist, fertile soil near a stream bank or in a woodland clearing, reflecting its natural preference for riparian and marginal woodland habitats throughout northern Eurasia. Bird cherry grows vigorously once established and will naturalize readily, so site it where suckering can be managed or is welcome as a thicket for wildlife. Expect the first flower racemes by year four to six, filling the garden with a heavy almond-like fragrance in mid to late spring before the leaves fully expand.

planting.note: Sow pits in fall at a depth of 1-2 inches to allow natural cold stratification over winter; seeds typically germinate reliably the following spring after the chilling requirement is met.

directions: Plant pits in fall in a sunny, well-drained spot with slightly acidic to neutral soil; this heat-tolerant native plum performs exceptionally well in the southeastern and south-central United States where many other plums fail. Thin seedlings to the strongest individual and allow it to develop naturally; wild goose plum will begin producing fruit within three to six years from a strong seedling and often spreads via root suckers to form a productive thicket. Cross-pollination with another wild plum or a compatible named variety will significantly improve fruit set and yields of the flavorful red-yellow fruits. This species is an important parent of many named southern plum varieties and provides early spring blooms that are a critical food source for native pollinators emerging from winter.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall to begin the warm-stratification phase naturally; seeds require a full warm period followed by a cold period, meaning germination typically occurs in the second spring after sowing.

directions: Sow cleaned seeds in fall and be patient, as double dormancy means most seeds will not sprout until the second spring; mark the bed clearly and keep it weeded during the waiting period. Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants are adaptable to a range of soil types and tolerate summer heat better than its European relative Cornus mas. The clusters of tiny yellow flowers that stud the bare branches in late winter are among the earliest blooms of the year, providing a vital nectar source for early pollinators; the cherry-like fruits that follow ripen to bright red by midsummer and are edible fresh or made into jams and wines. With age, the exfoliating bark develops an ornamental patchwork of grey, brown, and orange, making this a genuinely four-season specimen plant for gardens across a wide range of climates.
sources: Dirr's Trees and Shrubs for Warm Climates
